industries.name: Fire & Life Safety Inspection
industries.notes: Fragmented field of regional shops that keep fire alarms, sprinklers, and extinguishers up to code for commercial buildings. The work is recurring and legally required, the owners are mostly tradespeople in their 50s and 60s, and almost no one has rolled them up. I'd want to be the buyer who keeps the technicians and the local reputation while finally putting real scheduling and software behind it. Tailwinds: tightening fire codes, mandatory recurring inspections, and an aging owner base with few successors.

buy_box.notes: Recurring inspection & testing contracts; licensed technicians.

industries.name: Medical Billing / RCM
industries.notes: Small revenue-cycle-management shops that handle billing and collections for independent medical practices. Practices hate billing and rarely bring it back in-house once they outsource it, so the revenue is sticky and referral-driven. The owners are often former practice managers nearing retirement with no obvious successor. Tailwinds: rising billing complexity, physician practices staying independent, and steady demographic demand for care.

buy_box.notes: Recurring percent-of-collections contracts; sticky once outsourced.

industries.name: Veterinary Practice Mgmt Software
industries.notes: Vertical software that runs the front and back office of independent veterinary clinics — scheduling, records, billing, reminders. Switching costs are high once a clinic runs on it, churn is low, and the independent-vet market is still huge and under-consolidated. I'd want to take a beloved but under-resourced product and give it the engineering and support it never had. Tailwinds: pet-spend growth, corporate consolidation pushing independents to differentiate, and cloud migration off legacy installs.

buy_box.notes: Sticky, recurring SaaS; high switching costs.

industries.name: HIPAA Compliance Software
industries.notes: Software that helps clinics and small hospital systems stay HIPAA-compliant — risk assessments, policy management, training, and audit trails. The smallest providers carry the most risk and can least afford a compliance team, which is exactly where a focused product wins. It is my dream to take one of these and scale it the right way across the country. Tailwinds: rising enforcement and breach penalties, the shift to cloud EHRs, and vendor-risk requirements pushing every practice toward real compliance tooling.

buy_box.notes: Recurring SaaS; clinics & small hospital systems.

industries.name: Commercial HVAC Services
industries.notes: Regional commercial HVAC service and maintenance companies on recurring contracts for offices, schools, and light industrial buildings. Maintenance agreements throw off predictable revenue, the trade is graying fast, and private equity has only touched the largest players. The plan is to keep the crews, lock in the service contracts, and add the back-office systems these shops never built. Tailwinds: aging equipment replacement cycles, energy-efficiency retrofits, and a shrinking pool of licensed technicians.

buy_box.notes: Recurring service & maintenance contracts; licensed techs.

industries.name: Legal Case Management Software
industries.notes: Case-management software for small law firms — the two- and three-lawyer shops that run on whatever actually fits how they work. Once a firm runs its matters, deadlines, and billing on a tool, it almost never leaves, and the small-firm market is enormous and poorly served. I'd want to take a product lawyers already trust and finally give it the support and roadmap it deserves. Tailwinds: solo and small-firm formation, cloud adoption in legal, and demand for built-in billing and getting-paid features.

industries.name: Independent Elevator Inspection
industries.notes: Third-party elevator inspection and testing. Attractive for the recurring, mandated work, but the field is heavily licensed, regionally consolidated, and conflict-of-interest rules limit who can inspect what. Passing for now. Tailwinds: mandated recurring inspections and aging equipment — but the moats favor incumbents.

history.reason: Too consolidated and license-gated; little room for a first-time buyer.
